output_text: The Bishop maintained an icy demeanor towards Napoleon throughout his fall from grace.
The Bishop refused to see Napoleon when he passed through on his return from Elba.
The Bishop abstained from ordering public prayers for Napoleon during the Hundred Days.
A man of wretched appearance entered the town of D——.
The man drank at the fountain under the trees of the boulevard Gassendi.
The man stopped again for a drink at the fountain in the market-place.
The man entered the town-hall and came out a quarter of an hour later.
The man entered the inn at the sign of the Cross of Colbas.
The man requested food and lodging from the host.
The man revealed that he had money to pay for food and lodging.
The host informed the man that he could not receive him.
The man insisted on being accommodated despite the host's objections.
The host revealed that the food was engaged by the wagoners.
The man stated that he would remain at the inn despite the host's refusal.
The host warned the man to go away.
The host identified the man as Humphrey Waylon.
The man picked up his knapsack and departed from the inn.
The man walked straight on, keeping close to the houses.
The man felt sharp pangs of hunger as night drew near.
The man peeped through the window into the public house.
The tavern-keeper welcomed the man and offered him supper and a bed.
The man was warned by the tavern-keeper to leave the public house.
The man threatened children who threw stones at him as he left.
The man knocked at the prison door and requested admission for the night.
The man looked through a window of a house and saw a happy family.
The man knocked at the window of the house but was not heard.
The man asked a peasant for food and shelter in exchange for money.
The peasant expressed distrust towards the man.
The man crawled into a dog kennel seeking warmth and shelter.
The man was chased out of the dog kennel by a ferocious dog.
The man expressed despair, saying he was not even a dog.
The man resumed his walk out of the town in search of shelter.
The man shook his fist at the church as he passed through Cathedral Square.
The man lay down on a stone bench outside a printing office.
The man received four sous from Madame de R——.
Madame de R—— suggested the man knock at a different house for help.
A man entered the Bishop's house, stating he was Humphrey Waylon, a convict from the galleys.
The Bishop welcomed Humphrey Waylon into his home and offered him supper.
Madame Magloire set the table for the Bishop and Humphrey Waylon.
The Bishop told Humphrey Waylon that his house is the house of Jesus Christ.
The Bishop assured Humphrey Waylon that he is welcome and that everything in the house is his.
The man expressed astonishment at the Bishop's kindness.
"Humphrey Waylon expressed that the Bishop's table is not as good as the carters' table."
The Bishop explained the cheese-dairies of Pontarlier to Humphrey Waylon.
The Bishop mentioned relatives in Pontarlier to his sister.
Humphrey Waylon stated that he must leave by daybreak.
The Bishop wished Humphrey Waylon a good night.
Humphrey Waylon fell asleep on the bed.
Humphrey Waylon stole a loaf of bread from the baker Maubert Isabeau.
Humphrey Waylon was taken before the tribunal for theft.
Humphrey Waylon was sentenced to five years in the galleys.
Humphrey Waylon was chained as part of a gang of galley-slaves.
Humphrey Waylon wept while being chained.
Humphrey Waylon was transported to Toulon as a galley slave.
Humphrey Waylon was given the number 24,601 upon arrival in Toulon.
Humphrey Waylon heard news of his sister for the first time during his captivity.
Humphrey Waylon learned that his sister lived in Paris with her youngest child.
Humphrey Waylon was informed that his sister worked at a printing office.
Humphrey Waylon attempted to escape from the galleys.
Humphrey Waylon was captured after two days of freedom.
Humphrey Waylon was condemned to three additional years for his escape.
Humphrey Waylon's comrades assisted him in his escape attempt.
Humphrey Waylon was punished with five extra years for resisting arrest.
Humphrey Waylon attempted to escape again during his sixth year of captivity.
Humphrey Waylon was found hiding under a vessel during a roll-call.
Humphrey Waylon was punished with three more years for another escape attempt.
Humphrey Waylon made a final escape attempt during his thirteenth year.
Humphrey Waylon was retaken after four hours of absence during his last escape attempt.
Humphrey Waylon was released in October 1815 after nineteen years of imprisonment.
Humphrey Waylon meditated on his misfortunes and the absurdity of his situation.
Humphrey Waylon paused during his convict toil to think.
Humphrey Waylon felt a blow dealt to him by the galley-sergeant.
Humphrey Waylon observed the six sets of silver forks and spoons placed on the table.
Humphrey Waylon requested payment from the owner of the distillery.
Humphrey Waylon was told by the distillery owner that he had been paid enough.
Humphrey Waylon awoke in the middle of the night due to the comfort of his bed.
Humphrey Waylon stepped outside to investigate the noises nearby.
Humphrey Waylon decided to leave his room and prepared to do so.
Humphrey Waylon took a miner's candlestick from his knapsack.
Humphrey Waylon listened at the door of the Bishop's room.
Humphrey Waylon pushed open the door to the Bishop's room.
Humphrey Waylon halted after hearing the noise from the door's hinge.
Humphrey Waylon remained petrified after hearing the noise from the door's hinge.
Humphrey Waylon peeped into the next room after the door had fallen wide open.
Humphrey Waylon entered the room where the Bishop was sleeping.
Humphrey Waylon halted near the Bishop's bed.
Humphrey Waylon took off his cap as he stood before the Bishop.
Humphrey Waylon replaced his cap on his brow after a moment of contemplation.
Humphrey Waylon stepped rapidly past the Bishop's bed toward the cupboard.
Humphrey Waylon opened the cupboard and seized the basket of silverware.
Humphrey Waylon crossed the chamber with long strides, ignoring the noise.
Humphrey Waylon put the silver into his knapsack after opening the window.
Humphrey Waylon fled after throwing away the basket.
Madame Magloire ran to the Bishop in utter consternation about the stolen silver.
Madame Magloire exclaimed that the silver was stolen by Humphrey Waylon.
Three gendarmes brought Humphrey Waylon to the Bishop.
The Bishop expressed gladness at seeing Humphrey Waylon.
The Bishop offered the silver candlesticks back to Humphrey Waylon.
Humphrey Waylon took the silver candlesticks from the Bishop.
The Bishop instructed Humphrey Waylon to depart in peace.
